title: Quiz: Boson Sampling
description: Test your understanding of boson sampling, its computational complexity, and experimental implementations

questions.question: What mathematical function determines the output probabilities in standard boson sampling?
questions.explanation: Boson sampling output probabilities are proportional to |Perm(U_S)|^2, where U_S is the submatrix of the unitary corresponding to the input and output mode configuration. The permanent (unlike determinant) has no sign factors in the sum.

questions.question: Why is computing the permanent of a matrix considered computationally hard?

options.text: It requires quantum computers to compute
It is #P-hard, believed harder than NP-complete problems
The matrix must be very large
It can only be computed approximately

questions.explanation: Valiant proved that computing the permanent is #P-hard, meaning it's at least as hard as counting the solutions to NP problems. This is believed to be intractable even for quantum computers, unlike determinant which can be computed in polynomial time.

questions.question: What is the main difference between Gaussian boson sampling (GBS) and standard boson sampling?

options.text: GBS uses a different linear optical network
GBS uses squeezed states instead of single photons as inputs
GBS can only be done at optical frequencies

questions.explanation: Gaussian boson sampling uses squeezed vacuum states as inputs instead of single photons. This has practical advantages (squeezed states can be generated deterministically) and the output distribution relates to the hafnian function instead of the permanent.

questions.question: Approximately how many photons are needed to achieve quantum computational advantage with boson sampling?
questions.explanation: The crossover point where boson sampling becomes intractable for classical computers is estimated at around 50-100 photons, depending on assumptions. Recent experiments like Jiuzhang (2020) claimed advantage with ~50-70 detected photons.

questions.question: What is the 'verification problem' in boson sampling?

options.text: Ensuring the optical components are properly aligned
Confirming the device samples from the correct (computationally hard) distribution

questions.explanation: The verification problem is fundamental: if the output distribution is truly hard to compute classically, how can we verify the quantum device is sampling correctly? This remains an open problem. Partial solutions include testing small instances and checking marginal statistics.

questions.question: Boson sampling is a universal quantum computing model that can solve any problem a quantum computer can solve.
questions.explanation: False! Boson sampling is explicitly NOT universal quantum computing. It's a restricted model that can only sample from a specific distribution. It cannot run arbitrary quantum algorithms like Shor's algorithm. However, it demonstrates quantum computational advantage for its specific task.

questions.question: What mathematical function appears in Gaussian boson sampling output probabilities (analogous to the permanent in standard boson sampling)?
questions.explanation: In Gaussian boson sampling, output probabilities are related to the hafnian of a matrix. The hafnian sums over all perfect matchings (ways to pair up indices), and is also computationally hard to compute - making GBS also a candidate for quantum advantage.

questions.question: Why must photons in boson sampling be highly indistinguishable?

options.text: Distinguishable photons would be absorbed by the network
The permanent formula only applies to indistinguishable bosons; distinguishability makes the problem classically easy
Detectors can only detect indistinguishable photons

questions.explanation: The quantum interference (and resulting permanent formula) only applies to perfectly indistinguishable bosons. Distinguishable particles don't exhibit the same interference - their statistics become classical and efficiently simulable. This is why >99% indistinguishability is required.
